sections.content: Python's DB-API 2.0 (PEP 249) is the JDBC of the Python world: one standard interface, many drivers. sqlite3 ships with the stdlib; for real databases pip install a driver — psycopg (PostgreSQL), mysqlclient (MySQL), asyncpg (async Postgres). On top sits SQLAlchemy, the JPA/Hibernate analog.

sections.java: // JDBC: Connection, PreparedStatement, ResultSet try (Connection c = DriverManager.getConnection(url); PreparedStatement ps = c.prepareStatement( "SELECT name FROM users WHERE id = ?")) { ps.setLong(1, id); ResultSet rs = ps.executeQuery(); if (rs.next()) name = rs.getString("name"); }
sections.python: # DB-API: connect -> execute -> fetch import sqlite3 with sqlite3.connect("app.db") as conn: cur = conn.execute( "SELECT name FROM users WHERE id = ?", (id,)) row = cur.fetchone() name = row[0] if row else None

sections.title: Use Placeholders — Never f-string Your SQL
sections.content: Building SQL with an f-string (f"... id = {id}") is SQL injection — the same reason you reach for PreparedStatement over Statement in JDBC. Always pass values as a separate params tuple: sqlite3 uses ? placeholders, psycopg uses %s. That %s is a DB-API placeholder, not Python string formatting — leave it literal and let the driver bind and escape the value.

sections.java: // Manual transaction control Connection c = ds.getConnection(); try { c.setAutoCommit(false); c.prepareStatement("INSERT ...").executeUpdate(); c.commit(); } catch (SQLException e) { c.rollback(); }
sections.python: # The connection is a context manager: # commit on success, rollback on exception with sqlite3.connect("app.db") as conn: conn.execute( "INSERT INTO users(name) VALUES (?)", ("Ada",)) # committed automatically at block exit # psycopg's connection works the same way

sections.java: // JPA entity + EntityManager @Entity class User { @Id @GeneratedValue Long id; String name; } User u = em.find(User.class, id); List<User> hits = em.createQuery( "FROM User WHERE name = :n", User.class) .setParameter("n", name).getResultList();
sections.python: # SQLAlchemy 2.0 ORM from sqlalchemy import select from sqlalchemy.orm import Session, Mapped, mapped_column class User(Base): __tablename__ = "users" id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True) name: Mapped[str] with Session(engine) as s: u = s.get(User, id) hits = s.scalars(select(User).where(User.name == name)).all()

sections.title: Engine = DataSource; Core vs ORM
sections.content: create_engine("postgresql+psycopg://...") builds a pooled engine — the connection-pool DataSource analog — that you create once and share. SQLAlchemy has two layers: Core (a composable SQL expression language, close to JDBC but type-safe and database-agnostic) and the ORM (mapped classes + Session, the Hibernate analog). Use the ORM for domain models, Core for bulk/ETL. For async, swap to create_async_engine + AsyncSession with asyncpg (Module 08).

sections.question: What is the correct, safe way to include a user-supplied id in a query?
options: execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?", (id,)) — a placeholder plus a params tuple
Escape the quotes in id manually first
sections.explanation: Pass the value through the driver's placeholder (? for sqlite3, %s for psycopg) with the value in a separate params tuple — the DB-API equivalent of a JDBC PreparedStatement. Interpolating the value into the SQL string (f-string or concatenation) is the classic SQL-injection hole.
